expanded_description: Understanding the three main categories of exceptions to the octet rule: incomplete octets (electron-deficient atoms), expanded octets (hypervalent atoms), and odd-electron molecules (free radicals), including when and why they occur.

core_concept.principle: The octet rule has three main exceptions: incomplete octets (atoms with fewer than $8e^-$, common for Be, B, Al), expanded octets (atoms with more than $8e^-$, possible for Period 3+ elements), and odd-electron molecules (free radicals with unpaired electrons).
core_concept.explanation: While the octet rule works well for most Period 2 elements (C, N, O, F), exceptions arise due to orbital availability and electron count. Incomplete octets occur when atoms lack enough electrons to complete an octet (e.g., `$	ext{BF}_3$` has `$6e^-$` on B). Expanded octets occur when central atoms from Period 3 or below use empty d orbitals to accommodate more than `$8e^-$` (e.g., `$	ext{SF}_6$` has `$12e^-$` on S). Odd-electron molecules have an odd total valence electron count, making an octet for all atoms impossible (e.g., `$	ext{NO}$` has `$11e^-$`).

image.alt: Diagram showing three types of octet exceptions: incomplete (BF3), expanded (SF6), and odd-electron (NO)
image.caption: Three octet exceptions: incomplete octet (`$	ext{BF}_3$`, `$6e^-$`), expanded octet (`$	ext{SF}_6$`, `$12e^-$`), odd-electron (`$	ext{NO}$`, `$11e^-$`)

sections.introduction: Some atoms are stable with fewer than `$8e^-$` in their valence shell. This typically occurs for elements in Group 2 (Be) and Group 13 (B, Al) which have few valence electrons to share.

key_concept.definition: An incomplete octet occurs when a central atom has fewer than `$8e^-$` in its valence shell, typically `$4e^-$` (Be) or `$6e^-$` (B, Al).
key_concept.context: These atoms are often electron-deficient and act as Lewis acids, seeking additional electron pairs to complete their octet.

principles.statement: Atoms with fewer than 4 valence electrons may not have enough electrons to form enough bonds to complete an octet.
principles.application: Explains stability of `$	ext{BeH}_2$` (Be has `$4e^-$`) and `$	ext{BF}_3$` (B has `$6e^-$`).
principles.example: `$	ext{BF}_3$`: B has 3 valence `$e^-$`, forms 3 bonds, total `$6e^-$` around B.
principles.analogy: Like a table with only 6 chairs instead of 8; it's stable but has room for more.

principles.statement: Atoms with incomplete octets are electron-deficient and tend to accept electron pairs from other molecules (Lewis acids).
principles.application: Explains reactivity of `$	ext{BF}_3$` with `$	ext{NH}_3$` to form `$	ext{F}_3	ext{B-NH}_3$` adduct where B completes octet.
principles.example: `$	ext{BF}_3 + 	ext{NH}_3 ightarrow 	ext{F}_3	ext{B-NH}_3$`: B goes from `$6e^-$` to `$8e^-$`.
principles.analogy: Like a magnet seeking metal; electron-deficient atoms seek electron pairs.

classifications.value: Often form compounds with `$4e^-$` around central atom
classifications.behavior: Seek to complete octet through coordinate covalent bonds or polymerization

examples.explanation: Be central with 2 Be-H bonds; Be has `$4e^-$`; incomplete octet
Be central with 2 Be-Cl bonds; Be has `$4e^-$`; gas phase monomer has incomplete octet

classifications.value: Often form compounds with `$6e^-$` around central atom
classifications.behavior: Stable as monomers but react readily with Lewis bases to complete octet

examples.explanation: B central with 3 B-F bonds; B has `$6e^-$`; classic incomplete octet example
Al central with 3 Al-Cl bonds; Al has `$6e^-$`; dimerizes to `$	ext{Al}_2	ext{Cl}_6$` to share electrons

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure for `$	ext{BF}_3$`
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for boron trifluoride (`$	ext{BF}_3$`) and explain why boron has an incomplete octet.

step_by_step.explanation: B central (less electronegative); connect 3 F atoms with single bonds

step_by_step.step: Add lone pairs to F atoms
step_by_step.explanation: Add 3 lone pairs (`$6e^-$`) to each F atom to complete their octets
B has only 3 bonds (`$6e^-$`); no electrons remain to form lone pair or double bond

step_by_step.calculation: Structure with incomplete octet is preferred (minimizes FC)

worked_examples.final_answer: `$	ext{BF}_3$`: B central with 3 single bonds to F; B has `$6e^-$` (incomplete octet); all formal charges = 0; B is electron-deficient
worked_examples.concept_applied: Group 13 elements often form stable compounds with incomplete octets (`$6e^-$`) when formal charges are minimized.

sections.key_concept: Elements from Period 3 or below can accommodate more than `$8e^-$` in their valence shell by using empty d orbitals for bonding, allowing them to form more than 4 bonds.

categories.description: Only elements from Period 3 or below (n `$\ge$` 3) can expand their octet.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Period 2 elements (C, N, O, F) have only s and p orbitals (`$2s, 2p$`); no d orbitals available. Period 3+ elements have empty d orbitals (`$3d, 4d$`, etc.) available for bonding.

examples.explanation: Can use `$3d$` orbitals; forms `$	ext{SF}_6$` with `$12e^-$` around S
No `$2d$` orbitals exist; cannot expand octet; max `$8e^-$`

categories.description: Expanded octets often occur when they reduce formal charges on the central atom.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Drawing single bonds only may leave central atom with high positive FC; forming double bonds (expanding octet) can reduce FC to zero.

examples.explanation: All single bonds: S FC = `$+2$`; 2 double bonds: S FC = `$0$` (expanded octet preferred)
All single bonds: P FC = `$+1$`; 1 double bond: P FC = `$0$` (expanded octet preferred)

categories.description: Certain elements frequently exhibit expanded octets in their compounds.
categories.detailed_mechanism: P, S, Cl, As, Se, Br, Xe, I commonly form hypervalent compounds with 10, 12, or even 14 valence electrons.

classifications.value: Central atom has 5 bonding pairs (or 4 bonds + 1 lone pair)

examples.explanation: P central with 5 P-Cl bonds; P has `$10e^-$`
S central with 4 S-F bonds + 1 lone pair; S has `$10e^-$`

classifications.value: Central atom has 6 bonding pairs (or 5 bonds + 1 lone pair, etc.)

examples.explanation: S central with 6 S-F bonds; S has `$12e^-$`
Xe central with 4 Xe-F bonds + 2 lone pairs; Xe has `$12e^-$`

classifications.value: Central atom has 7 bonding pairs (or 6 bonds + 1 lone pair)
characteristics: Less common, typically Cl, I, Xe

examples.explanation: I central with 7 I-F bonds; I has `$14e^-$`
Cl central with 3 double bonds + 1 single bond; Cl has `$14e^-$`

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure for `$	ext{SF}_6$`
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for sulfur hexafluoride (`$	ext{SF}_6$`) and explain why sulfur can have an expanded octet.

step_by_step.explanation: S central (less electronegative); connect 6 F atoms with single bonds

step_by_step.step: Add lone pairs to F atoms
step_by_step.explanation: Add 3 lone pairs (`$6e^-$`) to each F atom to complete their octets
S has 6 bonds (`$12e^-$`); exceeds octet; this is allowed for Period 3+ elements
S is Period 3 with empty `$3d$` orbitals available for bonding; can accommodate more than `$8e^-$`
step_by_step.calculation: Expanded octet possible for Period 3+ elements

worked_examples.final_answer: `$	ext{SF}_6$`: S central with 6 S-F single bonds; S has `$12e^-$` (expanded octet); all atoms have FC = 0; S can expand octet because it's Period 3 with available d orbitals
worked_examples.concept_applied: Period 3+ elements can expand octet using d orbitals; expanded octets often minimize formal charges.

sections.key_concept: Molecules with an odd total number of valence electrons cannot satisfy the octet rule for all atoms; at least one atom must have an unpaired electron and an incomplete octet.

process_flow.description: Sum valence electrons from all atoms; if total is odd, octet rule cannot be satisfied for all atoms.
Connect atoms with single bonds; central atom is usually the less electronegative element (except H).
Complete octets on terminal atoms first; remaining electron(s) go on central atom.
Try to get central atom as close to octet as possible (7`$e^-$` is common for radicals).
process_flow.result: Best possible structure with unpaired electron

process_flow.description: Note which atom has the unpaired electron; this makes the molecule highly reactive.

classifications.value: Common odd-electron molecules involving nitrogen and oxygen
classifications.behavior: Unpaired electron makes them paramagnetic and reactive; often dimerize to pair electrons

examples.explanation: 11 valence `$e^-$`; N has 7`$e^-$`, O has 8`$e^-$`; unpaired electron on N
17 valence `$e^-$`; N has 7`$e^-$`; dimerizes to `$	ext{N}_2	ext{O}_4$` (18`$e^-$` per N unit)

classifications.value: Odd-electron molecules involving chlorine and oxygen
classifications.behavior: Unpaired electron on central Cl atom; highly reactive

examples.explanation: 19 valence `$e^-$`; Cl has 7`$e^-$`; unpaired electron on Cl

classifications.value: Odd-electron molecules are typically highly reactive due to unpaired electron
classifications.behavior: React readily with other molecules to pair the unpaired electron

examples.explanation: 2 `$	ext{NO}$` `$ightarrow$` `$	ext{N}_2	ext{O}_2$` (pairs unpaired electrons)
2 `$	ext{NO}_2$` `$ightarrow$` `$	ext{N}_2	ext{O}_4$` (pairs unpaired electrons)

worked_examples.title: Drawing Lewis Structure for `$	ext{NO}$`
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for nitric oxide (`$	ext{NO}$`) and explain why it violates the octet rule.

step_by_step.explanation: Connect N and O with a bond; odd electron count means octet rule cannot be satisfied for both atoms
Add lone pairs to complete O octet first (more electronegative); remaining electron goes on N
step_by_step.calculation: O gets 3 lone pairs (`$6e^-$`); `$3e^-$` left; N gets 1 lone pair (`$2e^-$`) + 1 unpaired electron

step_by_step.step: Form multiple bond to minimize formal charges
step_by_step.explanation: Form N=O double bond; N has 7`$e^-$` (5 from bonds + 2 from lone pair); O has 8`$e^-$`

step_by_step.calculation: FC: N = `$+1$`, O = 0; sum = `$+1$` (but molecule is neutral; unpaired electron accounts for difference)

step_by_step.explanation: Odd electron count (11`$e^-$`) makes it impossible for both atoms to have octets; one atom must have incomplete octet
step_by_step.calculation: `$	ext{NO}$` is a free radical with unpaired electron

worked_examples.final_answer: `$	ext{NO}$`: N=O double bond with unpaired electron on N; N has 7 valence electrons (incomplete octet); O has 8 valence electrons (complete octet); odd electron count makes octet rule impossible to satisfy for both atoms
worked_examples.concept_applied: Odd-electron molecules (radicals) cannot satisfy octet rule for all atoms; one atom must have incomplete octet.

sections.key_concept: A systematic approach helps identify when octet exceptions are necessary and which type of exception applies.

components.definition: Identifying octet exceptions requires checking total electron count, central atom period, and formal charge minimization.
components.equation: Check: (1) Odd `$e^-$` count? `$ightarrow$` Radical. (2) Period 2 central atom? `$ightarrow$` No expansion. (3) Period 3+ central atom? `$ightarrow$` Expansion possible.
components.interpretation: Systematic checking prevents errors like expanding octets for C, N, O, F or missing radicals.

image.alt: Flowchart showing decision process for identifying octet exceptions
image.caption: Decision tree: Odd `$e^-$` `$ightarrow$` Radical; Period 2 `$ightarrow$` No expansion; Period 3+ `$ightarrow$` Expansion possible

principles.statement: First check if total valence electron count is odd; if yes, the molecule is a radical and cannot satisfy octet for all atoms.
principles.application: Immediately identifies `$	ext{NO}$`, `$	ext{NO}_2$`, `$	ext{ClO}_2$` as exceptions before drawing structure.
principles.analogy: Like checking if you have an odd number of socks; one must be unpaired.

principles.statement: Check the period of the central atom; Period 2 elements cannot expand octet, Period 3+ can.
principles.application: Prevents invalid structures like `$	ext{CF}_5$` or `$	ext{OF}_6$`.
principles.example: `$	ext{SF}_6$`: S is Period 3 `$ightarrow$` expansion allowed; `$	ext{OF}_6$`: O is Period 2 `$ightarrow$` expansion forbidden.
principles.analogy: Like checking room size: small room (Period 2) fits 8 people max; large room (Period 3+) fits more.

principles.statement: For Period 3+ central atoms, expand octet if it reduces formal charges significantly.
principles.application: Explains why `$	ext{SO}_4^{2-}$` has double bonds (S FC = 0) rather than all single bonds (S FC = `$+2$`).
principles.example: `$	ext{SO}_4^{2-}$`: Expanded octet structure minimizes FC.
principles.analogy: Like rearranging furniture to make everyone more comfortable; expand octet to minimize charge stress.

worked_examples.title: Decision Process for `$	ext{PCl}_5$`
worked_examples.problem: Use the systematic approach to determine if `$	ext{PCl}_5$` requires an octet exception.

step_by_step.explanation: P is Period 3 (has `$3d$` orbitals available)
If P has octet, it can only form 4 bonds; but there are 5 Cl atoms
P forms 5 bonds to 5 Cl atoms; P has 10`$e^-$`

step_by_step.calculation: All FC = 0; expanded structure is optimal

worked_examples.final_answer: `$	ext{PCl}_5$` requires expanded octet (10`$e^-$` on P) because P is Period 3 and must bond to 5 Cl atoms; all formal charges = 0
worked_examples.concept_applied: Systematic check: even `$e^-$` count, Period 3 central atom, 5 bonds required `$ightarrow$` expanded octet necessary.

sections.key_concept: Misunderstandings about octet exceptions can lead to incorrect Lewis structures. Clarifying these concepts builds accurate understanding of bonding limitations.

classifications.type: Error: Expanding Octet for Period 2 Elements
classifications.value: Giving C, N, O, or F more than 8 electrons
characteristics: Using d orbitals for Period 2 elements
classifications.behavior: Remember: Period 2 elements have no d orbitals; max 8`$e^-$` (4 bonds)

examples.explanation: Wrong: C with 5 bonds (10`$e^-$`); Correct: C with 4 bonds (8`$e^-$`); C is Period 2, cannot expand
Wrong: N with 5 bonds; Correct: `$	ext{NF}_5$` does not exist; N is Period 2, cannot expand

classifications.value: Keeping Period 3+ central atoms to octet when expansion reduces formal charges
characteristics: Drawing `$	ext{SO}_4^{2-}$` with all single bonds
classifications.behavior: For Period 3+ central atoms, expand octet if it significantly reduces formal charges

examples.explanation: Wrong: all single bonds (S FC = `$+2$`); Correct: 2 S=O double bonds (S FC = 0, expanded octet)
Wrong: all single bonds (P FC = `$+1$`); Correct: 1 P=O double bond (P FC = 0, expanded octet)

classifications.value: Trying to force octet on molecules with odd total valence electrons
characteristics: Adding extra electron to make count even
classifications.behavior: If total valence `$e^-$` is odd, one atom must have unpaired electron and incomplete octet

examples.explanation: Wrong: forcing both N and O to have 8`$e^-$`; Correct: N has 7`$e^-$`, unpaired electron
Wrong: forcing all atoms to have octets; Correct: N has 7`$e^-$`, unpaired electron

classifications.value: Mixing up electron-deficient (incomplete) with hypervalent (expanded) atoms
classifications.behavior: Incomplete = fewer than 8`$e^-$` (Be, B); Expanded = more than 8`$e^-$` (Period 3+)

examples.explanation: Incomplete octet (6`$e^-$` on B), NOT expanded
Expanded octet (12`$e^-$` on S), NOT incomplete

sections.introduction: Test your understanding of octet exceptions with these practice problems. Attempt each before reviewing the solution.

key_concept.definition: Practice reinforces identification of incomplete, expanded, and odd-electron exceptions.
key_concept.context: Focus on applying systematic approach to various molecules.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 1
worked_examples.problem: Explain why `$	ext{BF}_3$` has an incomplete octet while `$	ext{SF}_6$` has an expanded octet.

step_by_step.explanation: B is Period 2, Group 13; has 3 valence `$e^-$`; forms 3 bonds (6`$e^-$` total)
step_by_step.calculation: B cannot expand octet (no d orbitals)

step_by_step.explanation: S is Period 3, Group 16; has 6 valence `$e^-$`; can use `$3d$` orbitals for bonding
step_by_step.calculation: S can expand octet (has d orbitals)

step_by_step.explanation: Period 2 elements (B, C, N, O, F): max 8`$e^-$`; Period 3+ elements (S, P, Cl, etc.): can exceed 8`$e^-$`
Incomplete octets common for Group 13 (B, Al); expanded octets only for Period 3+ elements
step_by_step.calculation: `$	ext{BF}_3$`: 6`$e^-$` on B (incomplete); `$	ext{SF}_6$`: 12`$e^-$` on S (expanded)

worked_examples.final_answer: `$	ext{BF}_3$` has incomplete octet (6`$e^-$` on B) because B is Period 2 and cannot expand octet; `$	ext{SF}_6$` has expanded octet (12`$e^-$` on S) because S is Period 3 with available d orbitals for bonding
worked_examples.concept_applied: Period 2 elements cannot expand octet (no d orbitals); Period 3+ elements can expand octet using d orbitals.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 2
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for `$	ext{ClO}_2$` and explain why it is a radical.

step_by_step.explanation: Cl (7`$e^-$`) + 2 `$	imes$` O (12`$e^-$`) = 19 valence electrons
Cl central with 2 O atoms; odd electron count means octet rule cannot be satisfied for all atoms
Add lone pairs to O atoms first; remaining electron goes on Cl
Odd electron count (19`$e^-$`) makes it impossible for all atoms to have octets; one atom must have incomplete octet
step_by_step.calculation: `$	ext{ClO}_2$` is a free radical with unpaired electron

worked_examples.final_answer: `$	ext{ClO}_2$`: Cl central with 2 Cl-O bonds and unpaired electron on Cl; Cl has 7 valence electrons (incomplete octet); odd electron count (19`$e^-$`) makes it a radical
worked_examples.concept_applied: Odd-electron molecules (radicals) cannot satisfy octet rule for all atoms; one atom must have incomplete octet.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 3
worked_examples.problem: Which molecule has an expanded octet: `$	ext{CF}_4$` or `$	ext{SF}_4$`? Explain.

step_by_step.explanation: C is Period 2; S is Period 3
step_by_step.calculation: C: no d orbitals; S: has d orbitals

step_by_step.explanation: C has 4 bonds (8`$e^-$`); octet satisfied; cannot expand
S has 4 bonds + 1 lone pair (10`$e^-$`); exceeds octet; expansion allowed
Only Period 3+ elements can expand octet
step_by_step.calculation: `$	ext{SF}_4$` has expanded octet; `$	ext{CF}_4$` does not

worked_examples.final_answer: `$	ext{SF}_4$` has an expanded octet (10`$e^-$` on S) because S is Period 3; `$	ext{CF}_4$` does not (8`$e^-$` on C) because C is Period 2 and cannot expand
worked_examples.concept_applied: Only Period 3+ elements can expand octet; Period 2 elements are limited to 8`$e^-$`.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 4
worked_examples.problem: Draw the Lewis structure for `$	ext{XeF}_4$` and determine the number of electrons around Xe.

step_by_step.explanation: Xe (8`$e^-$`) + 4 `$	imes$` F (28`$e^-$`) = 36 valence electrons
Xe central with 4 Xe-F bonds; complete F octets

step_by_step.step: Place remaining electrons on central atom
step_by_step.explanation: 36 - 32 = 4`$e^-$` remaining; add as 2 lone pairs on Xe
Xe has 12 valence electrons (expanded octet)

worked_examples.final_answer: `$	ext{XeF}_4$`: Xe central with 4 Xe-F bonds and 2 lone pairs; Xe has 12 valence electrons (expanded octet); square planar geometry
worked_examples.concept_applied: Noble gases (Period 5+) can form compounds with expanded octets; Xe has 12`$e^-$` in `$	ext{XeF}_4$`.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 5
worked_examples.problem: Explain why `$	ext{NO}$` is more reactive than `$	ext{N}_2$`.

step_by_step.explanation: `$	ext{NO}$` has 11 valence `$e^-$` (odd); unpaired electron makes it a radical
`$	ext{N}_2$` has 10 valence `$e^-$` (even); all electrons paired in triple bond
Unpaired electron in `$	ext{NO}$` seeks to pair; `$	ext{N}_2$` has strong triple bond and no unpaired electrons
step_by_step.calculation: `$	ext{NO}$` much more reactive than `$	ext{N}_2$`

step_by_step.explanation: Radicals (odd `$e^-$`) are highly reactive; molecules with all paired electrons are more stable

worked_examples.final_answer: `$	ext{NO}$` is more reactive than `$	ext{N}_2$` because `$	ext{NO}$` has an unpaired electron (radical, 11`$e^-$`) that seeks to pair, while `$	ext{N}_2$` has all electrons paired in a strong triple bond (10`$e^-$`)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Odd-electron molecules (radicals) are highly reactive due to unpaired electron seeking to pair.

summary: The octet rule has three main exceptions: (1) Incomplete octets: atoms with fewer than 8`$e^-$` (typically Be with 4`$e^-$`, B/Al with 6`$e^-$`); common for Group 2 and 13; act as Lewis acids. (2) Expanded octets: atoms with more than 8`$e^-$` (10, 12, or 14`$e^-$`); only possible for Period 3+ elements (P, S, Cl, Xe, etc.) using empty d orbitals; often minimize formal charges (e.g., `$	ext{SF}_6$`, `$	ext{SO}_4^{2-}$`). (3) Odd-electron molecules (radicals): molecules with odd total valence electrons (e.g., `$	ext{NO}$` 11`$e^-$`, `$	ext{NO}_2$` 17`$e^-$`); cannot satisfy octet for all atoms; highly reactive due to unpaired electron. Systematic approach: (1) Check electron count (odd `$ightarrow$` radical); (2) Check central atom period (Period 2 `$ightarrow$` no expansion, Period 3+ `$ightarrow$` expansion possible); (3) Minimize formal charges (expand octet if it reduces FC). Common errors include: expanding octet for Period 2 elements (C, N, O, F cannot expand), not expanding when appropriate for Period 3+, ignoring odd electron counts, and confusing incomplete with expanded octets. Mastery enables accurate Lewis structures for electron-deficient, hypervalent, and radical species.
